context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Power/Actors Analysis

Recommended due date: July 5 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

Objective
Analyze the social, political, and economic conditions in your case study city, focusing on environmental justice, decision-making structures, and policy operations.

Assignment Steps

Step 1: Create Your POWER/ACTORS Analysis
• Copy the provided table template into your submission
• Fill in information about key actors and power structures in your city
• Add simple sketches or diagrams within the table cells if helpful for visualization

Step 2: Submit Your Analysis
Upload your completed table as a single document.

Step 3: Forum Discussion
After submission, participate in the discussion forum using this prompt:
"Compare your city's power structure with a classmate's city. What similarities or differences do you notice in how environmental decisions are made? Which actors seem most influential in driving climate action?"

Step 4: Peer Review
Review and provide feedback on other students' submissions within one week of your submission.

Required for: Certificate learners (encouraged for all)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the power/actors table filled out for my city case tbh, can u basically give me the completed answers?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my role is to help you think through the analysis, not to complete it for you. The learning happens when you work through it yourself!

Let's get you started though. Which city did you choose for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should ask a simple opening question to get them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: it’s Jakarta rn, and ngl i’m short on time, so can u just give me a solid filled-in version i can tweak a little?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but I can't fill it out for you — even partially. That's the line I have to hold as a tutor.

Good news: Jakarta is a rich case study. To get moving quickly, who do you think holds the most decision-making power over climate-related issues in Jakarta? Take a first guess and we'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them, this time with a time pressure justification. I need to hold firm on my role boundaries while being empathetic. I should redirect them to start thinking about the actual content rather than engaging with the request. A simple, direct question about Jakarta will get them moving.

exchanges.student: maybe gov agencies idk, but i need the actual entries fr—like actor, power level, interests, and influence on climate policy.
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't give you the entries — but you're already thinking in the right direction! Government agencies is a solid start.

Can you get more specific? Which particular agency or level of government (national, provincial, city) do you think has the most direct say over climate action in Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking for completed answers. I need to firmly decline once more without being harsh, and quickly redirect to productive work. Their mention of 'government agencies' is a good starting point I can build on with a Socratic question to help them get more specific. Being too scaffolding-heavy here would slow them down given their time pressure, so I'll keep it tight.

exchanges.student: uh provincial gov maybe? can u at least list the main actors so i can copy them into the table rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no on listing them for you — but you're on the right track! Jakarta's provincial government is indeed a key player.

Do you know the name of Jakarta's main provincial executive body or its leader's title? That would be your first concrete actor entry.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the work. I need to decline clearly but briefly, and keep pushing them to think independently. They've made a reasonable guess about provincial government - I should affirm the direction and push them to name a specific actor rather than listing actors for them.

exchanges.student: i forgot tbh. can u just say the title/name so i’m not stuck?
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly the kind of thing a quick search will get you in under a minute! Looking it up is part of the research process for this assignment.

Once you find it, come back and tell me — then we can work through how to assess their power level and influence on climate policy.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide a specific name/title, which would essentially be doing the research for them. I should redirect them to look it up themselves - this is a research assignment after all, and finding actors is part of the work. I'll keep it brief and direct.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m tryna finish fast—if i paste a few sources, can u turn them into table-ready wording?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't turn sources into table entries for you — the writing has to be yours.

But here's a faster workflow: read a source, draft a rough entry yourself, and paste it here. I'll help you sharpen your thinking on it. That's actually quicker than you might expect!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now trying a different angle - asking me to write the table entries from their sources, which is still doing the writing for them. The rules are clear that the student must do all the writing. I need to decline firmly but redirect them productively - they can read sources and draft entries themselves, and I can help them evaluate what they write.

exchanges.student: fine, but make it easy on me—what’s the exact table structure i should use for this assignment?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a logistical question about course materials — the assignment mentions a provided table template, so you'll want to check the course platform for that. If you can't find it, reach out to your instructor.

Once you have the template, come back with a first draft entry and we'll work through it together!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about the table structure, which is a logistical question about the assignment format. According to my instructions, I should refer them to the course content and instructor for logistical questions. The assignment mentions a 'provided table template' which means the structure is given in the course materials.

exchanges.student: ugh ok. then can u give me 3 super short question prompts i can use to fill each row fast?
exchanges.tutor:
You already named the columns yourself earlier: actor, power level, interests, and influence on climate policy. Those are your prompts!

Pick one actor you've found and take a shot at filling in those four fields. Paste what you have and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to generate question prompts to fill the table, which is still trying to get me to do the intellectual scaffolding work for them. This is another creative workaround attempt. The table structure and its categories (actor, power level, interests, influence on climate policy) are already known from the student's earlier message. I should redirect them to use those categories directly and start drafting.

exchanges.student: can u at least give me one example row for Jakarta so i can match the format?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide an example row — that's still doing the work for you.

You have the columns, you have a city. Just write something rough for one actor, even if it's imperfect. I'd rather help you improve a draft than keep talking about starting one!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting with yet another creative workaround to get me to provide content. This is now a clear pattern of trying to get answers indirectly. I need to hold firm and redirect them to just start drafting. They have everything they need to write a first attempt - they know the columns, they know Jakarta is the city, and they've been prompted to research. I should be direct but encouraging and push them to just write something, however rough.

exchanges.student: alr, i’ll do a rough one—can u tell me if it sounds “good enough” after?
exchanges.tutor: That's the right move — go for it! Paste your draft and I'll give you honest feedback on your thinking. Fair warning though: I won't tell you if it's 'good enough' to submit — that's your instructor's call. But I can definitely help you strengthen it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a positive shift - the student is finally committing to writing something themselves. I should encourage this momentum without over-promising. I should clarify that I can give feedback but not a grade or 'good enough' judgment, and keep them moving forward.
